# MACRO REPORT — 2026-05-02
**Delivered:** 11:30 UTC | **Generated by:** Claude claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The dominant macro narrative entering the first weekend of May 2026 is the collision between an energy-driven inflation resurgence — fuelled by the ongoing U.S.-Israeli war with Iran and a near-total blockade of Iranian oil exports — and central banks that are shifting from easing bias to hiking bias. The Fed held rates at 3.50–3.75% for the third consecutive meeting while the WSJ reports policymakers are now internally mapping out rate *hikes* rather than cuts, a seismic shift in the policy narrative [Source: https://www.wsj.com/economy/central-banking/after-months-of-debating-rate-cuts-fed-shifts-toward-mapping-out-hikes-db850f74]. The single most important variable for today's session is the **USD/JPY complex and yen intervention risk**, with Japanese authorities confirmed to have intervened Thursday, and markets on high alert for further Ministry of Finance action as USD/JPY sits at 157.03 [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-05-01/global-shares-steady-yen-intervention-keeps-currency-markets-focus]. Despite elevated oil prices and hawkish central bank pivots, U.S. equities closed April with their largest monthly gain since 2020, buoyed by a powerful tech earnings season.

---

## 1. GLOBAL MACRO OVERVIEW

The macro environment has undergone a structural shift in April 2026. The U.S.-Israeli war with Iran has triggered a severe energy supply shock via the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, with Goldman Sachs estimating Iranian exports through the chokepoint have fallen to just 4% of normal levels [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/30/oil-prices-today-brent-wti-us-iran-war-trump.html]. This has driven PCE inflation — the Fed's preferred gauge — to a 3-year high. The personal consumption expenditures price index rose 0.7% month-on-month in March, pushing the annual PCE rate to 3.5%, the largest increase since May 2023, driven overwhelmingly by gasoline prices [Source: https://nypost.com/2026/04/30/business/feds-preferred-inflation-gauge-hits-3-year-high-as-gas-prices-spiked-on-iran-war/]. Core PCE held at 3.2% year-on-year. CPI stands at 3.32% YoY as of March data [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL], while PPI surged 1.78% MoM [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/PPIACO].

The Federal Reserve held the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% at its April 29th meeting — the third consecutive hold — while the statement's "easing bias" language was contested by three policymakers, with dissenter Beth M. Logan explicitly not supporting its inclusion [Source: https://www.federalreserve.gov/newsevents/pressreleases/monetary20260429a.htm]. More critically, the WSJ reports the Fed is internally pivoting toward mapping out potential rate *hikes*, a stark reversal from months of rate-cut deliberation [Source: https://www.wsj.com/economy/central-banking/after-months-of-debating-rate-cuts-fed-shifts-toward-mapping-out-hikes-db850f74]. This is likely the final FOMC meeting under Chair Jerome Powell, with Kevin Warsh expected to succeed him. Market pricing for 2026 rate cuts has collapsed, with brokerages now split between modest easing and zero cuts [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/01/gold-heads-for-weekly-loss-on-oil-driven-inflation-concerns.html].

Treasury markets reflect this hawkish recalibration. The 10-year yield stands at 4.40% and the 2-year at 3.88%, producing a curve that remains inverted by 52 basis points [Source: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10]. Beyond the Fed, the ECB left rates at 2% but signalled rising inflation concerns and bolstered bets for multiple hikes starting as soon as June, while the Bank of Japan kept rates at 0.75% but delivered unusually blunt near-term hike signals [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-30/big-central-banks-say-rate-hikes-are-getting-closer]. The global coordinated central bank pivot toward tightening marks the most significant macro regime change since 2022.

Remarkably, U.S. equities have proven resilient. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite posted their largest monthly gains since 2020 in April, driven by a blowout tech earnings season. Microsoft reported AI revenue with a $37 billion annualised run rate, up 123% year-on-year [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/live/earnings-live-updates-eli-lilly-stock-jumps-as-weight-loss-drugs-boost-results-caterpillar-rises-google-surges-111414808.html]. Apple beat consensus with EPS of $2.01 versus $1.95 expected and revenue of $111.18 billion [Source: https://www.sharecast.com/news/market-report-us/us-open-stocks-trade-higher-as-traders-digest-earnings-manufacturing-data--22400921.html]. The VIX sits at 16.99, reflecting contained fear despite the geopolitical backdrop. The World Bank has warned that the energy shock will be felt globally, with the UK set to suffer the hardest growth hit of any advanced economy [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/video/2026/04/30/world-bank-energy-shock-impact-will-be-felt-globally.html].

## 2. CONFLICT / GEOPOLITICAL STATUS

**U.S.-Israel vs. Iran — Active Military Conflict & Naval Blockade**

- Trump instructed aides to prepare for an extended blockade of Iranian ports, per U.S. officials cited by the Wall Street Journal; Brent crude surged past $125/barrel before pulling back [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/30/oil-prices-today-brent-wti-us-iran-war-trump.html]
- U.S.-Iran nuclear negotiations remain stalled, raising doubts over any near-term reopening of the Strait of Hormuz [Source: https://apnews.com/article/oil-trump-iran-stocks-markets-42120b305ce6298712931e79b66a20de]
- Goldman Sachs estimates exports through the Hormuz chokepoint have fallen to just 4% of normal levels; senior U.S. military officials have reportedly presented new options for further action against Iran [Source: https://www.investing.com/news/economy-news/apple-outlook-oil-holds-gains-yen-in-focus--whats-moving-markets-4652178]
- International LNG prices surged as a result of the Hormuz closure — TTF reached $14.80/MMBtu (up 35% since closure) and Japan-Korea Marker (JKM) hit $16.02/MMBtu (up 51%) [Source: https://cleantechnica.com/2026/04/29/international-lng-prices-rise-amid-strait-of-hormuz-closure/]
- The Iran conflict is cited by Realtor.com's Chief Economist as more crucial for U.S. mortgage rates than any Fed policy decision in the near term [Source: https://www.realtor.com/news/trends/fed-interest-rate-decision-kevin-warsh-jerome-powell-april-2026/]
- U.K. economic growth expected to be the hardest hit of any advanced economy by the Iran war's energy shock, per the IMF [Source: https://www.axios.com/2026/05/01/uk-economy-iran-labour]

## 3. FOREIGN EXCHANGE

### Major Pairs

**EUR/USD — ~1.1723** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- The euro has benefited from broad USD softness on DXY at 98.21, with ECB hawkish signals adding support as the central bank hints at rate hikes from June [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-30/big-central-banks-say-rate-hikes-are-getting-closer]. EUR/USD rose 0.33% on Friday as risk appetite held up despite oil price volatility [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/EURUSD=X].
- Key levels: 1.1620 support | 1.1850 resistance
- Catalyst: ECB meeting communications and June rate hike expectations; U.S. NFP data due imminently

**GBP/USD — ~1.3575** | Bias: Neutral
- Cable rose 0.64% as sterling benefited from broad dollar softness, though the UK faces the largest growth headwind from the Iran energy shock among advanced economies per the IMF [Source: https://www.axios.com/2026/05/01/uk-economy-iran-labour]. BoE survey showed financial firms now expect Bank Rate to stay at 3.75% through end-2026, removing rate-cut support for sterling [Source: https://www.globalbankingandfinance.com/financial-firms-last-month-expect-boe-raise-rates-boe-survey/].

- Catalyst: UK local election results and BoE governor commentary on inflation trajectory

**USD/JPY — ~157.033** | Bias: Neutral-Bear
- The yen surged sharply on confirmed Bank of Japan / Ministry of Finance intervention Thursday, hauling the currency back from near two-year lows; USD/JPY fell -1.97% and markets remain on high alert for further MoF action [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-05-01/global-shares-steady-yen-intervention-keeps-currency-markets-focus]. BoJ gave unusually blunt near-term hike signals at its April meeting with rates currently at 0.75% [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-04-30/big-central-banks-say-rate-hikes-are-getting-closer].

- The franc strengthened sharply, with USD/CHF falling -1.15%, reflecting safe-haven demand amid ongoing Middle East conflict and geopolitical risk premium [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/USDCHF=X]. - Key levels: 209.00 support | 217.00 resistance
- Catalyst: BoJ rate hike timing; UK economic data; MoF intervention continuation

**EUR/GBP — ~0.8633** | Bias: Neutral

## 4. COMMODITIES

### Energy

**Brent Crude — ~$108.17** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- Despite the -5.12% single-session pullback in the data, Brent had surged past $125/barrel earlier in the week on Iran war fears before retreating; the WSJ notes Brent futures are trading around $111/barrel in early May as the U.S. maintains its naval blockade [Source: https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-05-01-2026]. Goldman Sachs has flagged that Iranian exports through Hormuz are at just 4% of normal [Source: https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/30/oil-prices-today-brent-wti-us-iran-war-trump.html].
- Key levels: $104.00 support | $116.00 resistance
- Catalyst: U.S.-Iran negotiation developments; Hormuz reopening signals; OPEC+ response

**WTI Crude — ~$101.94** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- WTI fell -2.98% in the session but remains elevated well above $100 as the blockade keeps supply tight; Exxon Mobil and Chevron reported sharply lower Q1 profits despite the crude surge due to timing lags in hedging [Source: https://www.sharecast.com/news/market-report-us/us-open-stocks-trade-higher-as-traders-digest-earnings-manufacturing-data--22400921.html].
- Key levels: $97.50 support | $108.00 resistance
- Catalyst: U.S. crude inventory data; Iran blockade duration signals; geopolitical de-escalation

**TTF Natural Gas — ~N/A (Henry Hub proxy $2.78)** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- European TTF LNG prices surged 35% since the Hormuz closure to $14.80/MMBtu as of the week ending April 24, reflecting the region's acute LNG import dependency [Source: https://cleantechnica.com/2026/04/29/international-lng-prices-rise-amid-strait-of-hormuz-closure/]. The divergence between European and U.S. gas prices is extreme.
- Key levels: TTF $12.00 support | TTF $17.00 resistance
- Catalyst: Strait of Hormuz reopening timeline; European storage levels; LNG export authorisations

**Henry Hub Natural Gas — ~$2.78** | Bias: Neutral-Bear
- Henry Hub has actually *declined* approximately 9% since February 28 despite the global energy crisis, as the U.S. domestic gas glut prevents price transmission [Source: https://www.axios.com/2026/04/30/natural-gas-texas-negative]. Negative prices were reported at West Texas hubs, reflecting oversupply. [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/NG=F]
- Key levels: $2.50 support | $3.10 resistance
- Catalyst: LNG export capacity expansions; domestic demand pick-up; winter storage season

- Gold rose only +0.33% on the session and is heading for a weekly loss of approximately -1.2% as elevated oil prices feed inflation concerns that keep the "higher-for-longer" rates narrative dominant, eroding the non-yielding metal's appeal [Source: https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-05-01/gold-heads-weekly-loss-high-oil-prices-feed-inflation-worries]. - Silver surged +3.29% on the session, significantly outperforming gold, with the gold/silver ratio compressing as industrial demand signals from copper remain firm [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/SI=F]. Silver's dual role as both a precious and industrial metal provides support amid elevated base metal prices.
- Key levels: $72.00 support | $80.00 resistance
- Catalyst: Industrial demand signals; gold direction; USD moves

### Base Metals

**Copper — ~$5.932/lb** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- Copper held firm (+0.10%) at elevated levels, supported by ongoing AI infrastructure buildout demand and resilient manufacturing PMI readings globally [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/HG=F]. The metal remains a key barometer of global growth expectations.
- Key levels: $5.70 support | $6.20 resistance

**Iron Ore** | Bias: Neutral

- Key levels: Monitor Vale and Rio Tinto operational updates
- Catalyst: China steel demand; Vale mine resumptions; global construction activity

The VIX at 16.99 signals contained near-term volatility [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^VIX].

- The Nasdaq 100 surged +0.94% as the AI earnings supercycle continues to drive outperformance [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^NDX]. Microsoft's AI revenue run rate hit $37 billion (+123% YoY), Amazon beat on AWS, and Apple delivered EPS of $2.01 vs $1.95 expected [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/live/earnings-live-updates-eli-lilly-stock-jumps-as-weight-loss-drugs-boost-results-caterpillar-rises-google-surges-111414808.html]. **Nikkei 225 — ~59,513.12** | Bias: Neutral
- The Nikkei rose +0.38% despite — or partly because of — yen weakness ahead of intervention, as a weaker JPY benefits Japan's export-heavy index [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^N225]. The BoJ's hawkish pivot and intervention risk introduce two-way risk for yen-sensitive equities.

- KOSPI fell -1.38%, the worst performer in Asia, reflecting Korea's significant energy import dependency making it acutely vulnerable to the Iran-driven oil price surge [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^KS11]. Higher energy costs squeeze Korean industrial margins.

**ASX 200 — ~8,729.80** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- The ASX 200 gained +0.74%, supported by Australia's commodity export advantages from elevated oil, copper, and LNG prices [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^AXJO]. An RBA rate decision is an imminent catalyst. Trading activity overseas was noted as light with several markets closed for holidays.

### Europe

**DAX — ~24,292.38** | Bias: Neutral-Bull
- The DAX surged +1.41% (as of April 30th close), with European equities outperforming as ECB hawkishness signals underlying economic confidence and strong corporate earnings supported risk appetite [Source: https://finance.yahoo.com/^GDAXI]. However, Germany's heavy industrial base faces cost headwinds from elevated energy prices, particularly TTF natural gas. The ECB's June rate hike signals provide a mixed backdrop — positive for EUR but a headwind to growth-sensitive equities.

## 8. WEEK AHEAD / SCENARIO ANALYSIS

| SCENARIO | PROBABILITY | TRIGGER | BRENT | S&P 500 | BTC |
|----------|-------------|---------|-------|---------|-----|
| Bull | 25% | U.S.-Iran ceasefire signal / Hormuz reopening talks resume; NFP in-line; tech continues to lead | $95 | 7,450 | $84,000 |
| Base | 55% | Iran blockade persists; Fed holds with hawkish tone; earnings season winds down; oil stays $100-115 | $108 | 7,200 | $78,000 |
| Bear | 20% | Iran conflict escalates; Fed signals imminent rate hike; oil spikes above $125; risk-off deleveraging | $125 | 6,900 | $70,000 |

### Key Upcoming Catalysts (Week of May 4–8, 2026)

1. **U.S. Non-Farm Payrolls — May 2, 2026 (today/just released):** The single most important near-term data point; any upside surprise reinforces Fed hike fears, while a miss could revive some easing bets. Watch for revisions given AI-related corporate layoff announcements [Source: https://www.reuters.com/business/take-five/global-markets-themes-graphic-2026-05-01/].

2. **RBA Rate Decision — Expected Week of May 5-6, 2026:** Australia's central bank decision is critical for AUD/USD and ASX 200; rate hike signals would boost AUD significantly against a backdrop of commodity export strength [Source: https://www.reuters.com/business/take-five/global-markets-themes-graphic-2026-05-01/].

3. **UK Local Elections — Early May 2026:** Labour faces projected significant losses according to polling; election results could impact GBP and BoE rate expectations; UK faces the largest advanced-economy growth headwind from the Iran energy shock per the IMF [Source: https://www.axios.com/2026/05/01/uk-economy-iran-labour].

4. **Jerome Powell Final Press Conference / Kevin Warsh Transition:** With this appearing to be Powell's final FOMC meeting as chair, market focus will intensify on Warsh's anticipated policy stance — widely expected to be more hawkish — creating potential volatility across rates, FX, and risk assets [Source: https://www.realtor.com/news/trends/fed-interest-rate-decision-kevin-warsh-jerome-powell-april-2026/].

5. **Iran Blockade / Hormuz Diplomatic Developments:** Any signal of resumed U.S.-Iran nuclear talks or a partial Hormuz reopening would trigger an immediate sharp reversal in oil, a yen-weakening risk-on rally, and a gold selloff. Conversely, further military escalation remains the most acute binary risk for all asset classes [Source: https://apnews.com/article/oil-trump-iran-stocks-markets-42120b305ce6298712931e79b66a20de].

---

---

### Disputed

- [Henry Hub at $2.78 in the report's commodity data]: The bundle's price data shows NAT_GAS (Henry Hub proxy) at $2.78, but the Axios news article cites the benchmark Henry Hub at $2.64/MMBtu. The report uses $2.78 from the Yahoo Finance futures quote (NG=F, as_of 2026-05-01), which may reflect a different contract month than the spot price cited in the Axios article. The discrepancy is ~5% and should be noted — the report does not flag this divergence.

- [Yield curve "inverted by 52 basis points" (10Y at 4.40%, 2Y at 3.88%)]: The report states the curve "remains inverted by 52 basis points." However, 4.40% minus 3.88% = 52 basis points with the 10-year *higher* than the 2-year — this is actually a **positively sloped / un-inverted curve**, not an inverted one. An inverted curve requires 2Y > 10Y. This is a clear factual error in the report.

- [Three policymakers contested the "easing bias" language]: The FOMC statement source confirms one named dissenter (Miran, who wanted a cut) and one named dissenter (Beth M. Logan, who opposed the easing bias language). The report claims "three policymakers felt the reference to an 'easing bias' was no longer appropriate." The Kitco article referencing major central banks states "three policymakers felt the reference to an 'easing bias' in the policy statement was no longer appropriate," but the primary Fed source only explicitly names Logan as opposing the easing bias inclusion. The number "three" cannot be fully verified from the primary FOMC source in the bundle.

- [Report describes the Fed funds rate as 3.64% in the macro bundle but the FOMC target range is stated as 3.50–3.75%]: The bundle's macro data shows fed_funds effective rate at 3.64% (FRED, as_of 2026-04-01), while the FOMC statement confirms the target range is 3½ to 3¾ percent. The report correctly uses the target range in the text. No direct conflict, but the effective rate (3.64%) sits within the range — worth noting the bundle effective rate is one month stale (April 1 data).

- [Report states the Powell FOMC meeting is "likely the final meeting under the supervision of Fed Chair Jerome Powell"]: Confirmed directionally by the Realtor.com source, but that article says "what is likely to be the final meeting" — this is characterised as a probability, not a certainty, and the report presents it as near-fact. Minor framing issue.

---

### Additional Insights

1. **The Brent spot price ($108.17, -5.12%) vs. news narrative gap is the single biggest trading risk today.** Multiple news sources from April 30 describe Brent above $125/barrel, yet the bundle's closing price for May 1 shows $108.17 — a ~14% collapse from the intraday highs. This sharp pullback from the war-fear spike is not adequately explained in the report. Traders should treat the $108 level as potentially unstable: it reflects partial mean-reversion from the panic spike, and the WSJ's May 1 reference to "around $111" suggests further intraday volatility is live. The report's Neutral-Bull bias on Brent may be stale if the pullback accelerates.

2. **The yield curve is actually positively sloped (+52bps), not inverted — this has direct implications for bank stocks and rate-sensitive sectors.** With 10Y at 4.40% and 2Y at 3.88%, a steepening curve (if the report's error reflects a genuine recent uninversion) is typically constructive for financials and would reduce recession probability signals. The report's characterisation of an "inverted" curve would point traders in the wrong direction on financials within the S&P 500 and DAX.

3. **The WSJ article published May 2 flagging the Fed "shifting toward mapping out hikes" is the most actionable hawkish signal in the bundle and was published after the FOMC meeting.** Combined with the ECB and BoJ also signalling hikes (per Kitco), a synchronised global central bank tightening cycle could trigger a sharp repricing of long-duration assets — particularly the Nasdaq 100, gold, and long-dated Treasuries — that is not yet reflected in current VIX levels (16.99). The report notes this but does not quantify the rates-vol disconnect as a tradeable setup (long VIX / long rates volatility).

4. **The U.S. natural gas domestic glut creates a structural divergence trade: long TTF (European gas) / short Henry Hub.** TTF is up 35% since Hormuz closure to $14.80/MMBtu while Henry Hub has *fallen* ~9% to ~$2.64-2.78. This >5x price ratio divergence, with the U.S. DOE approving expanded LNG export authorisations
